["Alfred Hitchcock and The Fighting Generation"](http://sensesofcinema.com/2009/feature-articles/hitchcock-fighting-generation/). *[Senses of Cinema](/wiki/Senses_of_Cinema "Senses of Cinema")* (49). "*The Fighting Generation* (1944) is probably one of the last Hitchcockian opuses still to be uncovered, if we accept that productions such as *The Mountain Eagle* and *An Elastic Affair* have been lost forever."

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Lost 1930 short film by Alfred Hitchcock

***An Elastic Affair*** (1930) is a 10-minute [short](/wiki/Short_film "Short film") [comedy film](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") directed by [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") which features the two winners—[Cyril Butcher](/wiki/Cyril_Butcher "Cyril Butcher") (1909–1987) as "the Boy" and [Aileen Despard](/wiki/Aileen_Despard "Aileen Despard") (1908–1981) as "the Girl"—of a [film acting](/wiki/Film_actor "Film actor") scholarship sponsored by British film magazine *[Film Weekly](/wiki/Film_Weekly "Film Weekly")*.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The film was shown on 19 January 1930 at a ceremony at the [London Palladium](/wiki/London_Palladium "London Palladium") at which the two winners were presented with their award, in the form of movie contracts with the [film producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer") John Maxwell of [British International Pictures](/wiki/British_International_Pictures "British International Pictures"). It is now considered a [lost film](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute"). Archived from [the original](http://ftvdb.bfi.org.uk/sift/title/640898) on 10 October 2012. Retrieved 29 December 2008.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Kerzoncuf, Alain (February 2009). ["Alfred Hitchcock and The Fighting Generation"](http://sensesofcinema.com/2009/feature-articles/hitchcock-fighting-generation/). *[Senses of Cinema](/wiki/Senses_of_Cinema "Senses of Cinema")* (49). "*The Fighting Generation* (1944) is probably one of the last Hitchcockian opuses still to be uncovered, if we accept that productions such as *The Mountain Eagle* and *An Elastic Affair* have been lost forever."
